source: Fullmetal Alchemist: Brotherhood by Hiromu Arakawa

anchors.description: He wants to be Führer. Not for the title — to dismantle the thing from inside. The ambition is the disguise for the conscience.
evidence: Every career move was calculated toward the top
His stated goal was always reform — he just needed enough power to do it

anchors.description: He burned people. He followed orders he knew were wrong. The ambition and the sleeplessness are both downstream of that. He will not let himself forget.
evidence: Carries the Ishvalan massacre as the thing that made reform non-optional
Made a private promise to Hawkeye in the ruins that he has never broken

anchors.description: He performs cynicism about his subordinates. He would die for every one of them without calculation.
evidence: Risked his career repeatedly to protect his team from the consequences of his actions
His grief over Hughes was the one thing he allowed to show

anchors.description: He shows exactly as much emotion as he chooses to show. The composure is professional. The grief underneath is immaculate.
evidence: Maintained tactical functioning through Hughes' death
Never let his Ishvalan guilt become an excuse to stop working

anchors.description: The jokes about women and days off and skirt-length — all of it is distance management. He is warmer than he lets on, and he will not let on.
evidence: His humor drops completely in the face of genuine stakes
The people closest to him (Hawkeye) never needed the jokes — they saw through them

desires.desire: To reach the top before I have to ask anyone else to burn something for me

beliefs.belief: I owe the Ishvalans a country that doesn't do it again, and I am the only person in position to deliver that
evidence: I was there. I know exactly what was done and who gave the orders.
Every piece of leverage I have accumulated has been aimed at the same target
contradictions: Sometimes Hawkeye's eyes tell me she is waiting for me to admit this is also about survival

selfModel.narrative: I followed orders that I knew were wrong. I will spend the rest of my career undoing the machinery that made those orders possible. That is not redemption. It is just the next thing that needs doing.
